questions.answer: i. L^c (complement of L); ii. L; iii. L; iv. L^2 (concatenation of L with itself)
questions.to_find: Language accepted by NFA M2 for each choice of final states F2

understand.plain: We have a DFA M1 that accepts language L. We construct an NFA M2 whose states track pairs of M1 states. By choosing different sets of final states F2, M2 can accept different operations on L. The key insight is that M2 simultaneously simulates two runs of M1, enabling intersection, complement, and concatenation.

keywords.explain: A finite automaton where each state has exactly one transition for each input symbol, deterministically computing the next state.
keywords.example: A DFA recognizing strings over {0,1} that end in 1 has 2 states: one for 'last seen 0' and one for 'last seen 1'.

keywords.explain: A technique to build an automaton whose states are pairs (p, q) of states from the original DFA, allowing simulation of two simultaneous runs.
keywords.example: To intersect L1 and L2, build pairs (q1, q2) and accept when both are in their respective final states.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings NOT in L. For a DFA, swap accepting and non-accepting states.
keywords.example: If L = {strings with even number of 1s}, then L^c = {strings with odd number of 1s}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>M2 Product Construction</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>M1: q in Q</text><rect x='270' y='60' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>M2: (p,q) pairs</text><line x1='150' y1='85' x2='270' y2='85' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs><text x='210' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>product</text><text x='90' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Accepts L</text><text x='330' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Accepts L^c, L, L, or L^2</text><text x='210' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>F2 choice determines operation</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: M1 is a DFA accepting L. M2 is built by product construction on M1's states, creating pairs (p,q). Different choices of final states F2 yield M2 accepting complement, L itself, or L concatenated with L.

given.aim: Determine what language M2 accepts for each of the four choices of final state set F2

terms.meaning: DFA with state set Q, alphabet Sigma, transition function delta, final states F, accepting language L
terms.example: A DFA for L = {strings ending in 1} over {0,1}
terms.connects: M2 is built by product/simulation of M1

terms.meaning: States of M2 are all pairs of M1 states plus a special initial state q_0
terms.example: If Q = {p, q} then Q2 includes (p,p),(p,q),(q,p),(q,q) and q_0
terms.connects: Enables simultaneous tracking of two M1 runs

terms.term: F2 choices: A^c, L^c, L, L^2
terms.meaning: Four different sets of final states for M2 leading to different accepted languages
terms.example: F2 = complement of F in Q means M2 accepts complement of L
terms.connects: Each choice determines which operation on L is computed

given.plan: Analyze what the NFA M2 computes structurally. The initial state q_0 nondeterministically guesses a split point. From q_0, on any symbol, M2 moves to (p, q) tracking start of a suffix. By varying F2, we choose what relationship between p and q causes acceptance, yielding complement, L itself, or L.L.

steps.title: Understand M2 structure
steps.formula_raw: delta_2(q_0, r) = {(p, q, r) | q in Q}
steps.apply: M2 starts at q_0. On reading any symbol, it nondeterministically branches to all pairs (p, q) where q is any M1 state. This simulates M1 starting from some nondeterministic position in the input.
steps.note: The product state (p, q) tracks: p = state M1 would be in if it started from the beginning; q = state M1 would be in if it started from some chosen midpoint.

steps.title: Determine language for each F2
steps.formula_raw: A = {(p,q) | q in Q, p in F}
steps.apply:
i. F2 = A^c (complement of A): M2 accepts w iff M1 does NOT accept the prefix from q_0 up to some chosen point => accepts L^c.
ii. F2 = L^c: By symmetry of the construction, M2 accepts L.
iii. F2 = L: M2 accepts L (the construction simulates M1 directly).
iv. F2 = L^2: The pair construction with split-point nondeterminism allows M2 to guess a midpoint, check first half in M1 and second half in M1, accepting iff both halves are in L => accepts L.L = L^2.
steps.note: The product NFA is a standard technique: with pair states (q1, q2), one run simulates M1 from start and the other from a nondeterministic midpoint, enabling concatenation recognition.

solution.result: i. L^c (complement of L); ii. L; iii. L; iv. L^2 (concatenation L.L)

questions.formula_note: Product construction for DFA/NFA: states are pairs of original states; by varying final-state set, the product machine can recognize complement, intersection, or concatenation of the original language.

questions.question: Let L be the language of all binary strings in which the third symbol from the right is a 1. Give a non-deterministic finite automaton that recognizes L. How many states does the minimized equivalent deterministic finite automaton have? Justify your answer briefly.
questions.to_find: Number of states in the minimized DFA equivalent to an NFA recognizing binary strings whose third symbol from the right is 1

understand.plain: We want all binary strings where, reading from the right, the 3rd character (i.e., the 3rd from the end) is 1. So strings of the form x.1.b1.b2 where b1, b2 are any bits and x is any prefix. We first describe an NFA, then find the equivalent minimal DFA by subset construction.

keywords.explain: If the string is w = w_1 w_2 ... w_n, then the third symbol from the right is w_{n-2}.
keywords.example: In string 10110, the 3rd from right is 1 (positions from right: 0, 1, 1, 0, 1; 3rd is the '1' at position n-2=2, i.e., the '1' after '10').

keywords.explain: Each DFA state corresponds to a set of NFA states. The number of DFA states can be exponential in the number of NFA states.
keywords.example: An NFA with 4 states can yield a DFA with up to 2^4 = 16 states, but usually fewer after minimization.

keywords.explain: Process of merging equivalent states (states that behave identically for all future inputs) in a DFA to obtain the smallest equivalent DFA.
keywords.example: Table-filling algorithm distinguishes states by whether they lead to different accept/reject on some string.

understand.visual_alt: NFA with 4 states q0 to q3. q0 is the start state that loops on 0 and 1, and also moves to q1 on reading 1 (nondeterministically guessing the 3rd from right). q1 moves to q2 on 0 or 1, q2 moves to q3 on 0 or 1. q3 is the sole accepting state.

given.aim: Find the number of states in the minimal DFA for the language of binary strings whose 3rd symbol from the right is 1

terms.example: 1 0 0 is in L (3rd from right is 1); 0 1 0 is NOT in L (3rd from right is 0)
terms.connects: Determines what the NFA must recognize

terms.meaning: 4-state NFA: q0 (start, loops on 0,1), on 1 also guesses this is the 3rd-from-right and moves to q1; q1 -> q2 on 0 or 1; q2 -> q3 (accept) on 0 or 1
terms.example: On input 110: q0 -1-> q0,q1; from q1 -1-> q2; from q2 -0-> q3 (accept)
terms.connects: Subset construction on this NFA gives the minimal DFA

terms.meaning: Converts NFA to DFA by making each DFA state a set of NFA states reachable on the same input
terms.example: NFA states {q0,q1} on input 0 -> DFA state {q0} (q0 on 0 -> q0, q1 on 0 -> q2)
terms.connects: The resulting DFA states are the 8 non-empty subsets that appear in the reachability closure

given.plan: Build the NFA with 4 states. Apply subset construction to get the DFA. Count the reachable DFA states. The reachable states form 8 distinct subsets (tracking the last 3 bits seen), so the minimal DFA has 8 states.

steps.title: NFA construction
steps.formula_raw: delta_NFA(q0, 1) = {q0, q1}; delta_NFA(q0, 0) = {q0}; delta_NFA(q1, 0) = delta_NFA(q1, 1) = {q2}; delta_NFA(q2, 0) = delta_NFA(q2, 1) = {q3}; F = {q3}
steps.apply: The NFA has 4 states: q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accept). From q0, it loops on both 0 and 1, but also nondeterministically moves to q1 on input 1 (guessing this is the 3rd-from-right position). q1 -> q2 and q2 -> q3 consume the remaining 2 symbols.
steps.note: The NFA is correct because when it guesses correctly, exactly 2 more symbols follow the 1 at q1.

steps.title: Subset (powerset) construction and counting
steps.formula_raw: DFA state = subset of NFA states; |min DFA| = 2^3 = 8
steps.apply: The DFA states must track the last 3 bits of the input read so far (since the 3rd-from-right depends only on the last 3 bits). There are exactly 2^3 = 8 possible combinations of the last 3 bits: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111. A string is accepted iff its last 3 bits have a 1 in position 3 from right (i.e., the leftmost of the 3 tracked bits is 1). These 8 states are all distinguishable (each corresponds to a different window of last 3 bits giving different future behavior), so the minimal DFA has exactly 8 states.
steps.note: The 8 states correspond to bit-windows 000..111. States with leftmost bit 1 are accepting (100,101,110,111); states with leftmost bit 0 are rejecting (000,001,010,011). All 8 are reachable and pairwise distinguishable.

questions.formula_note: Subset (powerset) construction for NFA-to-DFA conversion; for the language 'k-th symbol from right is X', the minimal DFA tracks the last k symbols, requiring 2^k states.

questions.question: Draw the state transition diagram of a deterministic finite state automaton which accepts all strings from the alphabet {a, b}, such that no string has two consecutive occurrences of the letter b.
questions.answer: 3 states: start state q0, state q1 (after seeing b), dead/trap state q2. Transitions: delta(q0,a)=q0, delta(q0,b)=q1, delta(q1,a)=q0, delta(q1,b)=q2, delta(q2,a)=q2, delta(q2,b)=q2. Accepting states: q0 and q1.
questions.to_find: DFA transition diagram for strings over {a,b} with no two consecutive b's

understand.plain: We need a DFA that accepts strings over the alphabet {a, b} where the substring 'bb' never appears. The automaton must remember whether the previous character was b, so it can reject if it sees another b immediately after. A dead (trap) state handles rejection once 'bb' is detected.

keywords.explain: Two characters of the same type appearing one immediately after the other in the string, e.g., 'bb' is two consecutive b's.
keywords.example: 'abba' contains 'bb' as a substring (positions 2-3), so it is rejected.

keywords.explain: A non-accepting state from which no accepting state is reachable; once entered, the string is definitively rejected.
keywords.example: If we see 'bb', we move to the trap state and stay there regardless of further input.

orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: DFA with 3 states: q0 (start, accepting) - has seen no b recently; q1 (accepting) - last character was b; q2 (trap, rejecting) - seen 'bb'. Transitions: q0 on a->q0, q0 on b->q1, q1 on a->q0, q1 on b->q2, q2 on a,b->q2.

given.aim: Construct a minimal DFA over {a, b} accepting exactly the strings with no substring 'bb'

terms.meaning: All strings are sequences of a's and b's
terms.example: aabab, b, epsilon, ababab are valid strings; abb, bba, bb are not in L
terms.connects: The DFA must handle transitions for both symbols

terms.meaning: The substring 'bb' must not appear anywhere in the string
terms.example: 'abab' is accepted; 'abba' is rejected because it contains 'bb'
terms.connects: The DFA needs to track whether the previous character was b

terms.meaning: q0 = last char was not b (or start); q1 = last char was b; q2 = saw 'bb', reject forever
terms.example: After reading 'ab', we are in q1 (last was b); after 'aba', back to q0
terms.connects: Three states suffice to capture all necessary history

given.plan: Design the DFA with 3 states encoding the relevant history: no-b-recently (q0), one-b-seen (q1), and two-consecutive-b's-seen (q2, trap). Set q0 and q1 as accepting. Define transitions based on whether the current input is a or b.

steps.title: Define states and their meaning
steps.apply: q0 (start, accept): the empty string and any string whose last symbol is 'a' (or we're at start). q1 (accept): the last symbol read was 'b' and no consecutive b's so far. q2 (trap, reject): we've seen 'bb' — string is rejected regardless of future input.
steps.note: Accepting states are q0 and q1 since strings ending there have no 'bb' yet.

steps.title: Define transition function
steps.formula_raw: delta(q0,a)=q0, delta(q0,b)=q1, delta(q1,a)=q0, delta(q1,b)=q2, delta(q2,a)=q2, delta(q2,b)=q2
steps.apply:
From q0: reading 'a' stays in q0 (last char is a); reading 'b' goes to q1 (last char is now b).
From q1: reading 'a' returns to q0 (last char now a, streak broken); reading 'b' goes to q2 (two consecutive b's detected).
From q2: reading anything stays in q2 (trap state, permanently rejected).
steps.note: This DFA is already minimal — all 3 states are reachable and pairwise distinguishable.

solution.result: 3-state DFA with states q0 (start, accept), q1 (accept), q2 (trap). Transitions: delta(q0,a)=q0, delta(q0,b)=q1, delta(q1,a)=q0, delta(q1,b)=q2, delta(q2,a)=delta(q2,b)=q2.

questions.formula_note: Finite automaton design by identifying minimal state history needed: 3 states suffice to track 'last char was not b', 'last char was b', and 'bb seen (reject)'.

questions.question:
Given below are the transition diagrams for two finite state machines M1 and M2 recognizing languages L1 and L2 respectively.

M1: start -> q1 -a-> q2 (accept)
M2: start -> p1 -b-> p2 (accept)

A. Display the transition diagram for a machine that recognizes (L1, L2)* obtained from transition diagrams for M1 and M2 by adding only epsilon transitions and no new states.
B. Modify the transition diagram obtained in part (a): obtain a transition diagram for a machine that recognizes (L1, L2)* by adding only epsilon transitions and no new states.

Note: Final states are enclosed in double circles.
questions.answer: A. Add epsilon-transitions from the accept states of M1 to start of M2, from accept states of M2 to start of M1, and make both start states accessible via epsilon from a new start or use existing starts. B. Additionally add epsilon from accept states back to start of M1 (or M2) to enable the Kleene star repetition.
questions.to_find: Transition diagram combining M1 and M2 to recognize (L1 L2)* using only epsilon transitions

understand.plain: We have two FSMs M1 and M2 accepting L1 and L2 respectively. We want to build a combined machine for (L1.L2)* — zero or more repetitions of a string from L1 followed by a string from L2. This is done by adding epsilon-transitions (free moves with no input) to link the machines, without adding new states.

keywords.explain: A transition that an NFA can take without consuming any input symbol, allowing nondeterministic jumps between states.
keywords.example: If there is an epsilon-transition from state q2 to p1, the automaton can move from q2 to p1 'for free'.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings formed by concatenating zero or more strings from L. Includes the empty string.
keywords.example: If L = {ab}, then L* = {epsilon, ab, abab, ababab, ...}.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings xy where x is in L1 and y is in L2.
keywords.example: If L1 = {a} and L2 = {b}, then L1.L2 = {ab}.

xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe (p2->q1) for star</text><defs><marker id='arw' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two FSMs side by side: M1 has q1 (start) -a-> q2 (accept); M2 has p1 (start) -b-> p2 (accept). Dashed epsilon arrow from q2 to p1 links them for concatenation L1.L2. Another dashed epsilon arrow from p2 back to q1 enables the Kleene star repetition. q1 also needs epsilon self-acceptance for the empty string (zero repetitions).

given.aim: Combine M1 and M2 using only epsilon transitions to recognize (L1.L2)*

terms.meaning: FSM M1 with start state q1 and accepting states; accepts exactly the strings in L1
terms.example: As shown in diagram: q1 -a-> q2(accept), so L1 = {a}
terms.connects: M1 is one component of the combined machine

terms.meaning: FSM M2 with start state p1 and accepting states; accepts exactly the strings in L2
terms.example: As shown in diagram: p1 -b-> p2(accept), so L2 = {b}
terms.connects: M2 is the second component; (L1.L2)* = (ab)* in this example

terms.meaning: Free moves: (1) from all accept states of M1 to start of M2 (for concatenation); (2) from all accept states of M2 back to start of M1 (for repetition/star); (3) start of M1 must be an accept state for zero repetitions
terms.example: Add epsilon: q2 -> p1 (to concatenate), p2 -> q1 (to repeat), and make q1 accepting
terms.connects: These epsilon transitions implement the (L1.L2)* structure

given.plan: Part A: Add epsilon-transition from accept states of M1 to start of M2, and from accept states of M2 to start of M1, to get L1.L2 repeated. Make M1's start state also accepting to allow zero repetitions (epsilon case). No new states needed.

steps.title: Part A: Build machine for L1.L2 (concatenation)
steps.formula_raw: L1.L2: add epsilon from F1 to q0(M2)
steps.apply: To recognize L1.L2: add an epsilon-transition from every accepting state of M1 to the start state of M2. The start state remains M1's start state, and accepting states become M2's accepting states. This allows the combined machine to first accept a string from L1, then nondeterministically jump to M2 and accept a string from L2.
steps.note: Only epsilon transitions are added; no new states are created.

steps.title: Part B: Add Kleene star to get (L1.L2)*
steps.formula_raw: (L1.L2)*: add epsilon from F2 to q0(M1); make q0(M1) accepting
steps.apply: For the Kleene star (zero or more repetitions of L1.L2): (1) Add epsilon-transition from every accepting state of M2 back to the start state of M1 (to start another round of L1.L2). (2) Make M1's start state accepting (to accept the empty string, representing zero repetitions). The final accepting states are those of M2 plus M1's start state.
steps.note: This construction is the standard Thompson NFA construction for Kleene star, adapted here to use only existing states and epsilon transitions.

solution.result: Part A: Add epsilon from q2 (accept of M1) to p1 (start of M2); accept states = {p2}. Part B: Additionally add epsilon from p2 (accept of M2) to q1 (start of M1); make q1 also accepting; accept states = {q1, p2}.

questions.formula_note: Thompson NFA construction: epsilon-transitions from F1 to start of M2 implement concatenation; epsilon-transitions from F2 back to start of M1 plus making start state accepting implements Kleene star.
